1: šim'û 'iyyîm 'ēlay — Listen, O coastlands, to me. The Servant opens the second Servant Song (vv. 1–6) by addressing distant peoples directly. mibbeṭen 'immî hizkkîr šĕmî — from my mother's womb he named my name — the verb hizkkîr (Hiphil of זָכַר, to cause to be remembered/named) echoes the prophetic calling of Jeremiah (Jer 1:5: bĕṭerem 'eṣṣārkā babbèṭen yĕda'tîkā). Pre-natal election signals a calling of cosmic scope.
2: wayyāśem pî kĕḥereb ḥaddāh — He made my mouth like a sharp sword. Two weapon metaphors in sequence: the sword (ḥereb ḥaddāh, חֶרֶב חַדָּה — sharp sword) and the polished arrow (ḥēṣ bārûr, חֵץ בָּרוּר — a clean, selected arrow). Both are concealed — the sword in his hand's shadow (bĕṣēl yādô), the arrow in his quiver. The divine word is the weapon; its power is latent, held in reserve until the appointed moment. Rev 1:16 applies sword imagery to Christ's word.
3: 'abdî 'attāh yiśrā'ēl — You are my servant, Israel, in whom I will be glorified. The identification of the Servant as Israel (yiśrā'ēl) in v. 3, followed by the Servant's mission to Israel in v. 5, creates an interpretive tension: the Servant is both identified with Israel and distinguished from it. This is not textual confusion but deliberate complexity — the ideal Israel within Israel who accomplishes what the nation failed to do. The Hithpa'el 'etpā'ār — I will be glorified — is reflexive: YHWH is glorified in the Servant's work.
4: wĕ'ānî 'āmartî lĕrîq yāga'tî — But I said, I have labored in vain. The Servant's complaint uses the Pi'el yāga'tî (from יָגַע, to toil/labor) — denoting exhausting exertion. tōhû wĕhébel — for emptiness and vanity — the same pair (תֹּהוּ וָהֶבֶל) appears in Gen 1:2 and Eccl 1:2. The Servant's response is the pivot: mišpāṭî 'et-YHWH wĕpĕullātî 'et-'ĕlōhāy — my justice is with YHWH and my reward with my God — entrusting the outcome to the divine court rather than human success metrics.
5: wĕ'attāh 'āmar YHWH yōṣĕrî mibbeṭen — And now YHWH who formed me from the womb to be his servant. The verb yāṣar (יָצַר — to form, the potter's verb) marks the Servant as YHWH's crafted instrument, not self-selected. The purpose: lĕšôbēb̠ ya'ăqōb 'ēlāyw — to bring Jacob back to him — gathering dispersed Israel. wĕ'ănikal wĕ'ĕlōhāy hāyāh 'uzzî — though Israel is not gathered, yet I will be honored... and my God will be my strength — the concessive clause acknowledges apparent failure; divine sustaining is the ground of endurance.
6: wĕnĕtattîkā lĕ'ôr gôyim — I will give you as a light for the nations. The Servant's mission expands: first to restore Israel (v. 5), now to encompass all nations. lĕ'ôr gôyim (לְאוֹר גּוֹיִם) directly echoes 42:6 — the two Servant Songs share this climactic phrase. The scope: lihyôt yĕšû'ātî 'ad-qĕṣēh hā'āreṣ — to be my salvation to the end of the earth. NT: Luke 2:32 (Simeon's canticle), Acts 13:47 (Paul applies this to the Gentile mission).
7: lĭmĕga'ăl-nepeš — to one deeply despised. The participial phrase mĕga'ăl-nepeš (Pi'el participle of גָּעַל + נֶפֶשׁ, lit. one whom the soul abhors) is extreme language of rejection. The Servant is also mĕtā'ăb̠ gôy — abhorred by the nation — and le'ebed mōšĕlîm — slave of rulers. The reversal is sovereign: kings and officials will rise and bow down (qûmû sārîm yišŏtaḥăwû) — because YHWH who is faithful (ne'ĕmān) has chosen the Servant.
8: bĕ'ēt rāṣôn 'ănîtîkā — In a time of favor I have answered you. The divine speech opens with a dual temporal phrase: 'ēt rāṣôn (time of favor/acceptance) and yôm yĕšû'āh (day of salvation). Paul quotes this in 2 Cor 6:2, applying it to the eschatological now of Christ's gospel. wĕ'eṣṣārkā wĕ'ettĕnĕkā lĭbrît 'ām — I will keep you and give you as a covenant for the people — brît 'ām (בְּרִית עָם, covenant of people) is unique to Isaiah (cf. 42:6); the Servant is the covenant-person, not merely covenant-bearer.
9: lĕ'emōr laʾăsûrîm ṣĕ'û — saying to the prisoners, 'Come out!'. The Qal imperatives ṣĕ'û (go out!) and higālû (appear!) are the Servant's proclamation to the bound. Those in darkness are commanded to show themselves — darkness (ḥōšek) is the prison condition, not merely metaphor. yir'û — they shall graze — shifts from prisoner to flock imagery; the restored exiles are pastured by the roadways (bĕkol-šĕpāyim mir'êhem).
10: lō'-yir'ābû wĕlō'-yiṣmā'û — They shall not hunger, and they shall not thirst. The double negative (lō'... wĕlō') is emphatic: absolute provision is assured. šārāb̠ (שָׁרָב — scorching heat, mirage) and šèmeš (sun) will not strike them — wilderness hazards negated. The verb yĕnahălēm (Pi'el of נָהַל, to lead/guide to water) appears: YHWH himself leads them to springs of water. Rev 7:16-17 draws on this verse directly for the multitude before the throne.
11: wĕśamtî kol-hāray laddārek — I will make all my mountains a road. The cosmic highway motif (cf. 40:3-4): mountains become roads, raised highways (mĕsillātay yĕrūmmûn). This is divine geographical transformation to facilitate return. The passive yĕrūmmûn (Pual of רוּם — to be lifted up) frames the landscape as cooperative with the divine purpose.
12: hinnē 'ēlleh mērāḥôq yāb̠ō'û — Behold, these will come from far away. The returnees are gathered from four directions: north (ṣāpôn), west (yām, sea), and the land of Sinim (סִינִים — variously identified as Aswan/Syene in Egypt [LXX reads Persians]). The fourfold ingathering signals universality.
13: rannû šāmayim wĕgîlî 'āreṣ — Sing, O heavens, and rejoice, O earth!. The double imperative summons all creation to celebrate. pĕṣĕḥû hārîm rinnāh — break forth, mountains, into singing!. The ground: YHWH has comforted (niḥam, Pi'el perfect) his people and shown compassion (yĕraḥēm) on his afflicted.
14: wattō'mer ṣiyyôn 'ăzāb̠anî YHWH — But Zion said, YHWH has forsaken me. The complaint opens with the perfect 'ăzāb̠anî (has forsaken) and šĕkēḥanî (has forgotten) — both perfects of completed action from Zion's perspective. This is the existential cry of exile: divine abandonment experienced as fact, not merely felt. The divine response in vv. 15-16 answers not with denial but with escalating analogy.
15: hătiškāḥ 'iššāh 'ûlāh — Can a woman forget her nursing child?. The rhetorical question assumes the answer: inconceivable. 'ûl (עוּל, nursing infant) from the same root as 'ălāh — to suck. But the argument escalates: gam-'ēlleh tiš-kāḥnāh — even if these were to forget — wĕ'ānōkî lō' 'eškāḥēk — I will not forget you. Crucially, raḥēm (רַחֵם, to have compassion) shares its root with reḥem (רֶחֶם, womb) — the womb-bond is the linguistic ground of divine compassion (raḥamîm).
16: hēn 'al-kappayim ḥaqqōtîk — Behold, I have engraved you on the palms of my hands. The verb ḥāqaq (חָקַק, Qal perfect) means to engrave or inscribe — not written in ink but cut into stone. Zion's walls (ḥômōtayik) are continually before YHWH. The idiom is of permanent indelible memory — the opposite of forgetting. Palms are the most exposed, most constantly visible part of the body; what is inscribed there is inescapable.
17: mĭharsāyik ûmĕḥarbĕ'ayik mimmēk yēṣē'û — Your builders will outrun your destroyers. The contrast: those who tear down (mĕḥarbĕ'ayik, from חָרַב — to lay waste) will leave, and builders (bōnāyik) will make haste. The Qal imperfect yēṣē'û — they go out — implies departure of enemies. Restoration begins in their absence.
18: ś'î-sābîb̠ 'ênayik ûrĕ'î — Lift your eyes, look around and see. All of them gather and come to Zion (kullām niqbĕṣû bā'û lāk). The clothing metaphor: kullām kakkallāh tilbāšîm — you shall put them all on as an ornament, like a bride. Returned children adorning their mother — Zion's restoration is expressed as bridal clothing.
19: kî ḥorbōtayik wĕšomĕmōtayik — For your waste places and your desolate places. The ruins of devastation (ḥorbōt) will now be too cramped for population. The Qal imperfect yĭhyû — will be — frames the reversal as certain future. wĕrāḥăqû bĕlā'ayik — those who swallowed you up will be far away.
20: 'ôd yō'mĕrû bĕ'oznayik bĕnê šikkulāyik — The children of your bereavement will yet say in your ears. Children born of exile (bĕnê šikkulāyim, from שִׁכֻּלִים — bereavement/childlessness) now cry out for space: ṣar lî hammāqôm gĕšāh-lî wĕ'ēšēbāh — the place is too cramped for me; make room for me to dwell. The paradox: the desolate exile produces a population abundance the land cannot hold.
21: wĕ'āmartî bĭlbāb̠ĕk mî yālad lî 'et-'ēlleh — Then you will say in your heart, who bore these for me?. Zion's bewildered question: she was bereaved, exiled, rejected — who are these returned children? The verbs stack up: šĕkûlāh wĕgalmûdāh gōlāh wĕsûrāh — bereaved and barren, captive and set apart. The astonishment of restoration is the point: YHWH's action defies Zion's expectations.
22: hinnē 'eśśā' el-gôyim yādî — Behold, I will lift my hand to the nations. The signal (nēs, נֵס — banner/standard) to nations and the ensign to peoples summons: wĕhēbî'û b̠ānayik bĕḥōṣen — they shall bring your sons in their bosom. The nations become servants of Zion's restoration — reversing the exile dynamic without coercion: they carry and bring.
23: wĕhāyû mĕlāk̠îm 'ōmĕnāyik — Kings shall be your foster fathers. The social hierarchy inverts: kings nurture ('ōmĕn, guardian/foster parent), queens nurse (mĕyaniqqôt). 'appayim 'ereṣ yišŏtaḥăwû-lāk — they will bow to the ground with their faces toward you — prostration with the face to the earth is the deepest obeisance. The recognition formula closes: wĕyāda'tî kî-'ănî YHWH — and you will know that I am YHWH.
24: hăyuqqaḥ mig-gibbôr malqôaḥ — Can the prey be taken from the mighty?. The rhetorical question anticipates a negative answer: of course not — ordinary captives cannot escape the tyrant's grip (wĕšib̠yê ṣaddîq yimmālēṭ, can the captives of the righteous escape?). YHWH's response in v. 25 overturns the assumption entirely.
25: wĕgam-šĕbî gibbôr yuqqāḥ — Even the captives of the mighty shall be taken away. The Niphal imperfect yuqqāḥ (from לָקַח) reverses the rhetorical expectation of v. 24. wĕ'et-yĕrîb̠ĕk 'ānōkî 'ārîb̠ — I myself will contend with those who contend with you — emphatic personal pronoun ('ānōkî) stresses divine first-person engagement; the legal verb rîb̠ (contend, bring suit) frames it as YHWH acting as Zion's advocate in the divine court.
26: wĕhakaltî 'et-mĕ'annayik 'et-bĕśārām — I will make your oppressors eat their own flesh. The hyperbolically grotesque image — oppressors consuming themselves (ka'āsîs, as new wine / like mead) — conveys the self-destructive collapse of the nations who crushed Zion. The chapter's closing recognition formula is the broadest yet: wĕyāda'û kol-bāśār kî 'ănî YHWH môšî'ĕk — all flesh will know that I am YHWH, your Savior. The Hiphil participle môšî'ĕk (from ישׁע, your Savior) closes the chapter on the universal acknowledgment that the God of Israel is the saving God of all humanity.
